title: practical english usage - Part 324
content:
Let me know if you should happen to need any help.

very common.

British English by using should before happen, but this is no longer In sentences with if or in case, the idea of by chance can be emphasised in vocabulary' •  484  here and there Don't stay there in the corner by yourself. Come over here and talk to us. -> not (    .. .-htFs-n&l there.) (on the telephone) 'Hello, is Tom there?' 'No, I'm sorry, he's not here.' We   use here for the place where the speaker/writer is, and there for other places.

484 here     and   there For the expression can’t help. . .ing, ►    4 IB.

Would you like to help pack?

Help can also be followed directly by an infinitive without an object.

Our  main task is to help the company (to) become profitable.

Thank you so much  for helping us (to) repair the car.

Can you help me (to) find my ring? I       Can you help me finding my ring?) After help, we  can use object + infinitive (with or without to).

'I gather you didn't like the party.' 'What makes you say that?

7 understand you're moving to a new job.' 'Yes, that's right.' gather. These are often used to check  information.

Some other verbs can be used like this. Common examples are understand and I see (that) the firemen are going on strike.

I hear (that) Alice is expecting a baby.

introduce pieces of news which  one has heard or seen.

The  present-tense forms I hear (that)... and I see (that) ... are often used to 482 hear,     see,    etc   with    that-clause For hear + object + infinitive/*ing, ►     1 1 0.

There are similar differences between see, look (at) and watch. ►    575 not                                                                not Listen! (     Listen to!) Listen to me! (     Listen me!) When there is no object, listen is used without to. Compare: 5 listen      and   listen      to not I can hear somebody coming, (     7 am hearing...) in British English (►  84).

something at the moment of speaking, can hear is often used, especially Hear is not usually used in progressive forms. To  say that one hears 4 hear      not   used    in  progressive         forms Did you hear / listen to the news yesterday?

- I wish I had more time to listen to the radio, (     .. . /   hear the radio.) Brendel play ...) not I once heard Brendel play all the Beethoven concertos, (     Lonce listened Io not (     .. .-Fwas hearing...) - When she arrived, I was listening to a recording of Brendel playing Beethoven.

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  485  high and tall arrangements; hire, in American English, normally means ‘ employ’ .

In American English, rent is the normal word for both longer and shorter There's a shop in High Street that hires/rents (out) evening dress.

Hire (out) and rent (out) can also mean ‘ sell the use of something’ .

I need to hire/rent a car for the weekend.

How much  does it cost to rent a two-room flat?

hire can both be used.

flat). For shorter periods (e.g. paying for a car, a boat, evening dress) rent and used for arrangements involving a long period of time (one rents a house or a Hire and rent can mean: ‘ pay for the use of something’ . In British English, rent is 1  hire     and   rent 486 hire,     rent     and   let not Alex has got beautiful long legs, (     .. .-teU-legs/) Parts of the body can be long, but not tall.

4 parts    of  the   body That shelf is too high for me to reach.  The clouds are very high today.

may be higher than her mother, although she is probably not taller.

We   use high to talk about distance above the ground. A  child standing on a chair 3  distance      above     the   ground I'm lm  93 tall. That tree is about 30m  high.

In measurements, we  use tall for people, but we  prefer high for things. Compare: 2  measurements The garden's got very high walls.

Mount Elbrus is the highest mountain in Europe.

In other cases we  usually prefer high.

There are some beautiful tall trees at the end of our garden.

How tall are you? (     Hew high are you?) or electricity pylons).

things which  are higher than they are wide (e.g. factory chimneys We use tall mostly for people, trees, buildings with many floors, and a few other 1  What    kind    of  things     are   tall?
